problem: Spanish has no distinction between V and B sounds - both are pronounced as B
tip: For V: bite your lower lip gently with upper teeth. For B: close both lips completely.
practice: Very big vans bring vegetables

problem: Spanish J is like the guttural H (jamás). English J is like 'dzh' sound (jump, job)
tip: Start with 'd' sound, then add 'zh' (like measure). The tongue starts behind upper teeth.
practice: John jumps on jelly jars at the jewelry store

problem: Spanish H is always silent. English H requires breath/aspiration (house, hello, happy)
tip: English H is pronounced with aspiration - breathe out while saying it. Feel air on your hand.
practice: Hello! Have you heard about the happy house on the hill?

problem: Spanish adds vowel to consonant endings. English -ED has three pronunciations depending on final consonant
tip: After -T or -D sounds: add /id/. After voiceless consonants: /t/. After voiced consonants: /d/.
practice: I played, you wanted, he watched the loved animals

problem: Spanish -S always sounds the same. English -S has three pronunciations based on preceding sound
tip: After voiceless sounds (p,t,k,f): /s/. After voiced sounds (b,d,g,v,z,m,n): /z/. After s,x,ch,sh: /iz/.
practice: The cats and dogs watch buses crossing bridges

topic: Short I vs Long EE sounds
spanish: Sonidos I corta vs EE larga
problem: Spanish has one 'i' sound. English has both short /ɪ/ (ship) and long /i:/ (sheep)
tip: Short I: mouth relaxed, teeth not spread. Long EE: mouth spread like smiling, tenser
practice: The ship full of sheep sailed to the beach. I will feel the seat.

problem: Spanish doesn't have consonant clusters like 'str', 'spr', 'sch', etc. Spanish adds a vowel before them
tip: DO NOT add a vowel before S+consonant clusters. Say them together: STRR-eet, SPR-ay, SCH-ool
practice: Strong Spanish students study streets in the school

problem: Spanish adds a vowel after final consonants. English final consonants stand alone
tip: Final consonants in English have NO vowel after them. Practice stopping your voice completely.
practice: I stopped at the plant. The list is complete.

topic: Schwa sound (the most common English sound)
spanish: Sonido Schwa (el sonido más común en inglés)
problem: Spanish doesn't have schwa. English uses this reduced 'uh' sound constantly in unstressed syllables
tip: Schwa = unstressed syllable with neutral 'uh' sound. Most English vowels in unstressed syllables become schwa.
practice: The problem about banana is the sofa focus

topic: R sound differences (English R vs Spanish R)
problem: Spanish has rolled R (rr) or single tap (r). English R is a retroflex approximant (tongue curled back)
examples_wrong: rojo English R (using Spanish rolled r)
tip: English R: curl your tongue back without touching roof of mouth. DO NOT roll or tap like Spanish.
practice: The red car runs in the rain on the right road

problem: Spanish has neither 'sh' sound nor distinct 'ch'. These sounds are similar and easily confused
tip: SH: tongue down, air through teeth and lips. CH: tongue up, small explosion of air.
practice: The ship has chairs. She chose to shop and wash

problem: Spanish Z is like 'th'. English Z is voiced (zoo) and S is voiceless (Sue), very different from Spanish
tip: Z: vocal cords vibrate, sound buzzes. S: no vibration, like air escaping.
practice: The zebra is in the zoo. Sue sips slowly

problem: Spanish requires double negatives (no quiero nada). English uses only ONE negative
pairs: can't see no one → can see anyone
tip: In English: one negative only. Choose EITHER the negative verb OR a negative pronoun, not both.
practice: I don't want anything. He doesn't know anybody. She can't see anyone.

problem: Spanish puts adjectives AFTER nouns (casa grande). English puts most adjectives BEFORE nouns
tip: In English: Adjective BEFORE noun (big house). In Spanish: Adjective AFTER noun (casa grande)
practice: The big house has a red door and beautiful gardens

topic: Missing subject pronouns (subject is required in English)
problem: Spanish omits subject pronouns (Es raining = It is raining). English requires explicit subjects
tip: English ALWAYS needs a subject: It is raining (not just 'Is raining'). I am tired (not just 'Am tired')
practice: It is raining. It is snowing. I am tired. You are happy.

problem: Spanish has SER (identity) and ESTAR (location/condition). English has only 'to be', causing confusion
pairs: Tengo hambre → I am hungry (not 'I have hunger')
tip: English doesn't distinguish ser/estar. Most Spanish 'ser' phrases become 'am' (I am a teacher). Most 'estar' become 'am' too (I am tired).
practice: I am a teacher. I am tired. I am interested. I am hungry.

problem: Spanish assigns gender to all nouns (la mesa = feminine). English uses 'it' for objects, not he/she
pairs: La mesa, ella es → The table, it is
El coche, él es → The car, it is
El libro, él es → The book, it is
tip: In English: ONLY people and animals get 'he' or 'she'. Objects ALWAYS get 'it'.
practice: The car is red. It is fast. The table is big. It is wooden.

problem: Spanish overuses present progressive. English uses simple present for permanent states/abilities
tip: Verbs about knowledge, feelings, possession do NOT use progressive: know, understand, have, like, want, need.
practice: I know English. I understand the problem. I have two brothers. I like pizza.

problem: Spanish prepositions differ from English. 'Depender de' is 'depend ON' not 'depend OF'
tip: English prepositions are not predictable from Spanish. Learn them as phrases: 'depend on', 'dream about', 'speak English'
practice: I depend on you. I dream about travel. I think about work. I speak English.

problem: Spanish can make questions with only intonation (¿Tú quieres café?). English requires verb-subject inversion
tip: English ALWAYS inverts subject and auxiliary verb: DO you...? ARE you...? HAS she...? Even with 'be': Are you ready?
practice: Do you like coffee? Are you ready? Has she finished? Will they come?

problem: Spanish has no 'th' sound. English has two: voiceless /θ/ (think, bath) and voiced /ð/ (this, mother)
examples_correct: this (put tongue between teeth, voice it)
tip: Put your tongue BETWEEN your teeth. Voiceless TH (think): no voice. Voiced TH (this): use your voice.
practice: I think this is the third thing. Thank you for the bath.
